20.1.label: Question 20.1
20.1.scenario: A health and care insurance company determines its supervisory reserves using a similar regime to Solvency II, ie using best estimate assumptions with no prudential margins. The company calculates its embedded value in accordance with European Embedded Value Principles, ie splitting it into free surplus, required capital and PVIF components.
20.1.question: N/A

(i).question: Explain the impact that a new regular premium critical illness insurance contract would have on the change in the company's embedded value over the year.

Impact on embedded value: increase assets by the premiums received during the year on this contract
reduce assets by the initial expenses
reduce assets by any other expenses incurred during the period between inception and year end (eg overheads, renewal costs if applicable)
decrease assets by the investment return earned on the (negative) net cashflows up to the end of the year (minor impact for CI insurance)

reduce the total supervisory reserves, as the best estimate liability for the contract should be negative; this is because the value of expected future premiums should outweigh the value of future expenses and benefits due to loadings for profit and initial expense recovery
increase capital requirements due to the extra capital required to cover the extra risk (allowing for any diversification benefits)
have no impact, as there are no prudential margins so there should be no PVIF element

unless there are contract boundaries imposed in the supervisory valuation which are not imposed in the embedded value calculation, or differences in the discount rate used eg via the use of a matching adjustment or similar, etc.

Overall, if the contract is written on a profitable basis, the reduction in supervisory reserve and increase in assets (from the initial premiums) should outweigh the reduction in assets (due to initial expenses) and the increase in required capital, resulting in an increase in embedded value over the year, ie an embedded value profit.

20.2.label: Question 20.2
20.2.scenario: As part of the process for monitoring a health insurance company's experience for its conventional long-term health insurance business, it performs an annual analysis of the change in embedded value of this business. The report has been submitted to the company's Board of directors. After reading the report, one non-executive director has written: "I am surprised that the expense and morbidity items in the change in embedded value profit are negative but our premium rates are unchanged. Please explain what is going on. If experience is worse than assumed, should you not increase the premiums immediately?"
20.2.question: N/A

(i).question: Set out the points that would be made in a reply.

Explanation of negative items: The fact that the expense and morbidity items are negative is worrying.

This is showing that claim inceptions are heavier than assumed and/or that claim amounts are more than assumed (eg because claimants are taking longer to recover in the case of income protection insurance).
Likewise, it is showing that expenses are higher than assumed, so provides some evidence that expenses are too high...
... especially if the company is adopting an active approach to the EV and so the basis does reflect current expense experience.
Change premium rates immediately?: For morbidity experience, the key thing is to get a best estimate of future experience.
a detailed claims experience investigation, split by policy class and duration
allowance for future trends based on the company's experience over a number of years, reinsurer's data, other published info etc.
For expenses, a detailed expense investigation would be required, splitting initial, renewal and claim expenses by class.
These expenses should be compared with the expense loadings in the premium rates.
Even if morbidity or expenses do differ from assumed, the company may not automatically change the rates:
consider the materiality of the difference and hence the amount by which premiums would change
we need to look at more than just one year in isolation and consider the long-term trend
what policy classes are affected - the policies may have been sold many years ago, so there may be nothing that can be done
consider competition and impact that the change would have on sales volumes.

20.3.label: Question 20.3
20.3.scenario: A health and care insurance company that is subject to the EU Solvency II regime has recently ceased to transact new business. The in-force business comprises unit-linked and conventional pre-funded long-term care insurance policies and critical illness insurance contracts. An actuarial consultant has been asked to determine the embedded value of the health and care insurer, to assist a potential purchaser in making an offer for the company. The purchaser has asked the consultant to determine the embedded value as the sum of a net assets component, defined as the excess of assets over both liabilities and solvency capital requirements, and a present value of the in-force business (PVIF) component.
20.3.question: N/A

(i).question: Describe how the consultant would determine suitable assumptions for use in the embedded value calculation in respect of: (a) expense inflation (b) lapse and surrender rates.

Determining suitable assumptions: In general, for a sale and purchase or a merger a best estimate basis should be used.
However, the purchaser will be keen not to use a basis that produces too high a value.
Any published information, eg an EEV or other EV published using an actively reviewed best estimate basis, may inform the basis choice.

Any method and basis they come up with in such circumstances must be supported by sound actuarial analysis, but they are likely to favour an approach and basis that produces a lower rather than higher value.
(a) Expense inflation: This would be based on a view of future inflation. This is influenced in part by history and must be consistent with the other economic assumptions.
The relationship between fixed-interest and index-linked bond yields may be used to give a market view of future inflation.
Expense inflation is influenced by the split between salary-related and retail inflation-related costs and also the age and efficiency of the company's computer and administration systems.
The consultant might allow for a lower rate if there are any expected synergies with the purchaser's company.
However, if the funds are to be kept separate, they might increase the inflation rate to allow for the shrinking volume of in-force business.
(b) Lapse and surrender rates: These would be influenced by the company's experience, if known to the potential purchaser.
Otherwise estimates based on any published data, eg Supervisory reports, regulator's persistency reports, press comment. This would also be based upon experience and judgement.
Sensitivity testing would be necessary, especially if the unit-linked LTCI charging structure is sensitive to this assumption (ie the surrender value is quite different from asset share).
Need to consider possible effects on the surrender experience of closing to new business. Higher rates are likely because the news of the closure may worry existing policyholders.

(ii).question: Describe the method they would use to calculate the embedded value as defined.

Method for EV calculation: The net assets will be the total assets in excess of those covering the technical provisions plus SCR under Solvency II.
As the business is 100% shareholder-owned, 100% of the market value of these net assets will be included in the embedded value (EV).
The PVIF is the present value of future shareholder cashflows projected to emerge from the in-force business.
Under Solvency II, the PVIF includes the release of the risk margin component of the technical provisions and the release of the SCR.
These amounts may be reduced to allow for the cost of holding this required capital, eg frictional costs such as taxation and agency costs.

The PVIF also represents the release of any margins if there are places where the assumptions underlying the best estimate liabilities are not in line with the company's best estimate assumptions, eg if the best estimate liabilities have been determined using a constrained discount rate or including premiums up to some restrictive contract boundary.
To calculate the PVIF, a model would be used to project future cashflows on the business and the future supervisory reserves and capital requirements.
In the case of the unit-linked LTCI business, both the unit and non-unit funds would be projected, as the non-unit cashflows depend on the size of the unit fund.
A set of assumptions will be needed to project the future cashflows, including investment returns, mortality, expenses, withdrawals, inflation and so on.
The current and likely future tax position of the company must also be considered. This will depend upon the company's mix of business. Tax should therefore be dealt with at a 'global' level.
These projections would enable the transfers to the shareholders in each future year to be determined.
The in-force policies may be modelled individually, but this may not be practical due to IT limitations or limited access to the data of the target company.
The in-force business could then be represented by a number of sample policies (model points).

The contracts will be split by class, most notably unit-linked and without-profits contracts treated separately. They will then be split into smaller groups, eg by product type, policy term, method etc.
The PVIF will be the present value of the shareholder transfer arising each year.
The discount rate may allow for a risk margin.

If the embedded value calculation is performed on a market-consistent basis, the investment return and discount rate assumptions will be based on risk-free rates, and separate allowance would need to be made to allow for non-hedgeable risks.

(iii).question: Discuss how they could treat recurrent single premium long-term care insurance policies in the embedded value calculation.

Recurrent single premium long-term care: Because the contracts are recurrent single premium, it is difficult to judge what future premiums under the contracts are likely to be.
There is scope for discussion as to whether the future premiums should be regarded as part of the in-force business.
This is similar to the issue of establishing the 'contract boundary' for these contracts for Solvency II purposes...
... although a different treatment may be adopted for this embedded value (where the treatment is unconstrained) than for Solvency II (which must comply with the regulations).
This gives a number of possible approaches, for example:

1. Treat as new business and therefore ignore future contributions to in-force business. As the purchaser this might be the preferred option, but it may be difficult to justify this. However, if this is the treatment adopted by the company under Solvency II, this may strengthen the argument for adopting this approach.

2. Allow for future premiums on some assumptions. These may be best estimate, although the purchaser may prefer prudent assumptions and so might assume high rates of withdrawal and of ceasing contributions.
